title: Sliding Window
summary: Efficiently compute properties of contiguous subarrays or substrings by maintaining a moving window rather than recomputing from scratch.
overview: The sliding window technique maintains a [left, right] range over a linear data structure. Instead of computing properties for every possible window from scratch (O(n²) or worse), you expand the window by moving right, and shrink it by moving left, maintaining an invariant. The total work is O(n).
coreConcept: A window has two pointers: left and right. Expand right to add elements. When the window violates the invariant (e.g., too many unique chars, sum too large), advance left to shrink it. The key insight: each element enters and exits the window at most once, so total operations are O(n) even with the inner while loop.
problemPatterns: Maximum/minimum sum subarray of fixed size k
Maximum number of items in a time window
signals: Maximize or minimize length of a range
Rate limiting: events within a time frame

frontendScenarios.title: Rate Limiter — Max Requests Per Window
frontendScenarios.description: Enforce 'max N requests per minute'. Use a sliding window of timestamps. On each request, add the timestamp and remove timestamps older than 1 minute. If window size > N, reject.

frontendScenarios.title: Viewport Buffering for Virtual Lists
frontendScenarios.description: A virtual list renders only visible items plus a buffer. The 'visible window' slides as the user scrolls. Compute which items to render by maintaining the window of indices within startIndex..endIndex.

dataThinking: Think of the window as a 'current view' that tracks a running aggregate (sum, count, frequency map). The invariant defines what makes a valid window. Expanding adds to the aggregate; shrinking removes from it. If you maintain the aggregate incrementally, each step is O(1).

naiveApproach.description: Nested loops: for each left pointer, iterate right to compute window properties.

betterApproach.description: Single pass with two pointers. Maintain aggregate incrementally as window expands/shrinks.
betterApproach.complexity: O(n) time, O(k) space where k is window size or alphabet size

visualizationSteps.label: Fixed window k=3, arr=[2,1,5,1,3,2]
visualizationSteps.explanation: Initial window [2,1,5], sum=8. This is our baseline maxSum.

visualizationSteps.label: Slide right: add 1, remove 2
visualizationSteps.explanation: Window moves to [1,5,1]. windowSum = 8 + 1 - 2 = 7. maxSum stays 8.

visualizationSteps.label: Final result
visualizationSteps.explanation: Done in O(n). Each element visited exactly once. Max sum subarray of size 3 is [5,1,3] = 9.

practiceItems.title: Minimum Window Substring
practiceItems.question: Given strings s and t, find the minimum window in s that contains all characters of t. Classic interview problem; in frontend it models finding the minimum DOM range containing all required elements.
practiceItems.hint1: Expand right until you have all chars of t in your window. Then shrink left as much as possible while still valid.
practiceItems.hint2: Track 'have' count vs 'need' count to know when the window is valid without iterating the freq map.

practiceItems.title: Sliding Window Rate Limiter
practiceItems.question: Implement a RateLimiter class that allows at most maxRequests requests per windowMs milliseconds. Each call to allow(timestamp) returns true/false. This is a real API gateway pattern.
practiceItems.hint1: Maintain a queue of timestamps. On each call, remove timestamps older than windowMs from the front.
practiceItems.hint2: After removing old timestamps, check if queue.length < maxRequests before adding the new one.

reflectionPrompts: Why is the sliding window O(n) even though it has a while loop inside the for loop?
How would you adapt a fixed-size window to a time-based window (events within the last N seconds)?
What makes a problem unsuitable for sliding window? (Hint: think about what properties the aggregate must have.)
In virtual list rendering, the 'window' isn't over an array but over a coordinate space. How does the concept still apply?
